content.problemStatement:
You are given two strings:


texttexttext (also called **haystack**)
patternpatternpattern (also called **needle**)
Your task is to determine the **index of the first occurrence** of patternpatternpattern in texttexttext.

If patternpatternpattern does not appear in texttexttext, return **-1**.

Function DeclarationFunction NameSearchSearchSearch – This function finds the **index of the first occurrence** of a substring within a given string.

Parameters
texttexttext : The main string (haystack) in which the pattern is searched.
patternpatternpattern : The substring (needle) to search for.
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the **0-based index** of the first occurrence of patternpatternpattern in texttexttext.
Returns **-1** if the pattern does not exist in the text.
Constraints
1≤∣text∣,∣pattern∣≤1051 \leq |text|, |pattern| \leq 10^51≤∣text∣,∣pattern∣≤105
Both strings consist of **lowercase English letters** (`'a'`–`'z'`) only

Input Format

The first line contains a string texttexttext.
The second line contains a string patternpatternpattern.


Output Format

Print a single integer — the index of the first occurrence of patternpatternpattern in texttexttext.
If the pattern is not present, print **-1**.
constraints: All characters are lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains the string text (haystack) and the second line contains the string pattern (needle). Both consist of lowercase English letters only.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer — the 0‑based index of the first occurrence of pattern in text, or -1 if it does not occur.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: 'ming' starts at index 7 in 'programmingisfun'.
Test case 2: 'tree' does not occur in 'datastructures', so -1.
Test case 3: 'll' starts at index 2 in 'hello'.
The single character pattern matches at index 0.
Pattern longer than any matching substring, so not found.

hidden.label: stress case 2
hidden.input:
3
aaaa
aa
abcd
e
hello
lo

hidden.label: stress case 3
hidden.input:
5
abc
x
hello

world

codechef
chef
test
testt

solution.approach: We use the Knuth‑Morris‑Pratt (KMP) algorithm. First the longest‑proper‑prefix which is also suffix (LPS) array of the pattern is built in O(m) time, where m is the pattern length. Then we scan the text with two indices, advancing the pattern index on matches and using the LPS array to skip unnecessary comparisons on mismatches. The first time the pattern index reaches m we have found the leftmost occurrence, and its start position is i‑m. If the scan finishes without a full match we output -1. This runs in linear time for each test case and uses only O(m) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|text| + |pattern|) per test case, O(total length of all test cases) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(|pattern|) auxiliary space for the LPS array.
